1:
"And Joseph was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him of the hands of the Ishmeelites, which had brought him down thither." The witnesses note Joseph brought down to Egypt and bought by Potiphar. On "Joseph was brought down to Egypt," the grammatical voices note the descent. Gill and Henry note that "Joseph was brought down to Egypt" — Joseph, sold by his brethren (37:28), was carried down to Egypt; the resuming of the Joseph narrative (after the Judah interlude, ch. 38); the providence bringing Joseph to Egypt (where God would raise him to save his house, 45:5-8); the rejected son in a strange land.

On "Potiphar... captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the master. Clarke and Gill note that Potiphar (an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, the executioner/bodyguard) bought Joseph from the Ishmaelites (37:28, 36); the Egyptian master; the providence placing Joseph in a great house; the slave bought into Potiphar's service.

On "bought him of the hands of the Ishmeelites," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: Joseph sold and bought (a slave); yet the providence of God overruling (the LORD with him, v.2); the descent that was the way to exaltation; the rejected son, by God's hand, brought to the place of his future glory (and the saving of many, 50:20). The verse displays Joseph brought down to Egypt and bought by Potiphar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the descent — "Joseph was brought down to Egypt," the sold son carried to Egypt (37:28, Gill, Henry); "Potiphar... bought him," the Egyptian master (37:36, Clarke, Gill); the providence overruling (45:5-8, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the descended Joseph. The verse marks the descent: to Egypt. The verse displays the Potiphar: his master. The verse displays the slave: bought. The verse displays the providence: at work. The descent displays Joseph brought to Egypt by God's hand. The verse displays the Joseph: brought down. The verse displays the Egypt: his place. The verse displays the providence: overruling. Joseph brought down to Egypt and bought by Potiphar displays the providence: Joseph, sold by his brethren (37:28), was bought by Potiphar in Egypt — the descent that was the way to exaltation (45:5-8; 50:20), the rejected son brought by God to the place of his future glory. The verse displays the enslaved Joseph, with whom the LORD was (v.2).
2:
"And the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian." The witnesses note the LORD being with Joseph in his servitude — the crux of God's presence. On "the LORD was with Joseph," interpreters expound the presence. The grammatical-historical and the dominant reading note the great recurring theme — "the LORD was with Joseph": God's gracious presence with the enslaved Joseph (the phrase repeated, vv.3, 21, 23; the keynote of the chapter and of Joseph's life, Acts 7:9, "God was with him"); not that Joseph's circumstances were easy (he was a slave, far from home, falsely accused, imprisoned), but that God was with him in them all — blessing, prospering, comforting, and protecting; the secret of his success and of his endurance; "it is God's presence that makes prosperous" — the LORD's being with him the source of all.

On "he was a prosperous man," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the prospering. Calvin and Gill note that Joseph "was a prosperous man" — God prospered him (in his service, in all he did, vv.3-5); the prosperity flowing from God's presence (not from favorable circumstances, but from the LORD with him); the blessing on the godly servant (even in slavery); the success that God gave.

On "in the house of his master the Egyptian," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the place: "in the house of his master the Egyptian" — Joseph served in Potiphar's house; the LORD with him even there (in a heathen house, a slave); the providence blessing him in the place of his servitude; the godly young man, away from home and the means of grace, yet not forsaken (God with him). The verse displays the LORD being with Joseph in his servitude.

The witnesses agree the substance: the LORD was with Joseph, prospering him in Potiphar's house (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's presence: "the LORD was with Joseph" — the keynote (vv.3, 21, 23; Acts 7:9), God's gracious presence with the enslaved, falsely-accused, imprisoned son, blessing and prospering him in all; not easy circumstances, but God with him in them; the secret of his success and endurance ("it is God's presence that makes prosperous"). The verse displays the presence crux. The verse marks the LORD: with Joseph. The verse displays the prospering: from God. The verse displays the house: of the Egyptian. The verse displays the presence: the secret. The presence displays God with Joseph in his servitude. The verse displays the LORD: present. The verse displays the Joseph: prospered. The verse displays the slavery: not forsaken. The LORD being with Joseph in his servitude displays the crux: the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man (vv.3, 21; Acts 7:9) — God's gracious presence with the enslaved son, blessing and prospering him in all, the secret of his success and endurance (God's presence makes prosperous). The verse displays the prospered Joseph, seen by his master (v.3).
3:
"And his master saw that the LORD was with him, and that the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand." The witnesses note Potiphar perceiving that the LORD prospered Joseph. On "his master saw that the LORD was with him," the grammatical voices note the perception. JFB and Gill note that "his master saw that the LORD was with him" — Potiphar (a heathen) perceived that the LORD (the God of Joseph) was with him; the blessing on Joseph so evident that even his pagan master saw it; the witness of the world to God's presence with the godly (as Laban owned of Jacob, 30:27); the divine favor manifest.

On "the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the prospering. Calvin and Gill note that "the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand" — God gave Joseph success in all his work; the prospering hand of God; the blessing that made Joseph valuable to his master; the evident favor of God on the servant's labors.

On the perception, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Potiphar's seeing the LORD with Joseph led to his promotion (v.4); the providence raising Joseph even in slavery; the world owning God's blessing on his servant; the favor that prepared Joseph's advancement. The verse displays Potiphar perceiving that the LORD prospered Joseph.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the perception — "his master saw that the LORD was with him," Potiphar owning God's presence (30:27, JFB, Gill); "the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand," the prospering of God (Calvin, Gill); the favor that led to promotion (v.4, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the perceiving Potiphar. The verse marks the seeing: the LORD with him. The verse displays the prospering: in his hand. The verse displays the master: owning it. The verse displays the favor: manifest. The perception displays the world owning God's blessing on Joseph. The verse displays the Potiphar: seeing. The verse displays the LORD: prospering. The verse displays the Joseph: favored. Potiphar perceiving that the LORD prospered Joseph displays the world's witness to God's presence: his master saw that the LORD was with him and prospered all he did (30:27) — the heathen master owning God's blessing on the godly servant, the favor that led to his promotion (v.4). The verse displays the favored Joseph, made overseer (v.4).
4:
"And Joseph found grace in his sight, and he served him: and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand." The very brief verse notes Joseph made overseer of Potiphar's house. The grammatical voices note the promotion. Gill and Clarke note that "Joseph found grace in his sight, and he served him: and he made him overseer over his house" — Joseph found favor with Potiphar (who saw God's blessing on him, v.3), served him personally, and was made overseer (steward) over the whole house; the rapid rise of the godly servant; the trust reposed in Joseph; the providence advancing him.

On "he made him overseer over his house," the witnesses note the trust. Gill notes that Potiphar made Joseph steward over his house — the high trust; the management of the whole estate; the favor that the blessing of God had won (v.3).

On "all that he had he put into his hand," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the charge: "all that he had he put into his hand" — Potiphar entrusted everything to Joseph; the complete confidence; the steward over all (the providence preparing Joseph for greater rule, ch. 41); the faithful servant trusted with all. The verse displays Joseph made overseer of Potiphar's house.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promotion — "Joseph found grace in his sight... and he made him overseer over his house," Joseph the steward (v.3, Gill, Clarke); "he made him overseer over his house," the trust (Gill); "all that he had he put into his hand," the complete charge (ch. 41, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promoted Joseph. The verse marks the grace: found. The verse displays the overseer: of the house. The verse displays the charge: all in his hand. The verse displays the trust: complete. The promotion displays Joseph advanced by God's favor. The verse displays the Joseph: overseer. The verse displays the house: in his charge. The verse displays the providence: advancing. Joseph made overseer of Potiphar's house displays the rise of the godly servant: Joseph found grace and was made overseer, all put into his hand (v.3) — the trust won by God's blessing, the providence preparing him for greater rule (ch. 41). The verse displays the trusted Joseph, with the blessing on the house (v.5).
5:
"And it came to pass from the time that he had made him overseer in his house, and over all that he had, that the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake; and the blessing of the LORD was upon all that he had in the house, and in the field." The witnesses note the LORD blessing Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake. On "the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Gill notes that "the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake" — God blessed Potiphar's house (and all his substance) for the sake of Joseph (the godly servant in it); the blessing that flows to a household for the sake of the righteous (as to Laban for Jacob, 30:27; Obed-edom for the ark, 2 Samuel 6:11); the presence of God's servant a blessing to the house.

On "for Joseph's sake," the reformed and witnesses note the godly. Gill notes that the blessing came "for Joseph's sake" — the righteous a blessing to those around them; God blessing the heathen master for the sake of his servant; the favor of God overflowing.

On "the blessing of the LORD was upon all that he had in the house, and in the field," the grammatical-historical note the extent: "the blessing of the LORD was upon all... in the house, and in the field" — the blessing on the whole estate (indoors and out, the household and the husbandry); the abundant favor; the prospering of Potiphar through Joseph; the providence enriching the master for the servant. The verse displays the LORD blessing Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake," the blessing for the godly servant (30:27; 2 Samuel 6:11, Gill); "for Joseph's sake," the righteous a blessing (Gill); "upon all... in the house, and in the field," the whole estate (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed house. The verse marks the blessing: for Joseph's sake. The verse displays the house: of the Egyptian. The verse displays the field: and house. The verse displays the godly: a blessing. The blessing displays the house prospered for Joseph. The verse displays the LORD: blessing. The verse displays the Joseph: the cause. The verse displays the estate: enriched. The LORD blessing Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake displays the godly a blessing to others: the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake, in house and field (30:27; 2 Samuel 6:11) — the presence of God's servant enriching the heathen master. The verse displays the prospered Potiphar, trusting all to Joseph (v.6).
6:
"And he left all that he had in Joseph's hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat. And Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured." The witnesses note Potiphar's complete trust in Joseph and Joseph's comeliness. On "he left all that he had in Joseph's hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat," the grammatical voices note the trust. Gill and Calvin note that "he left all that he had in Joseph's hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat" — Potiphar entrusted everything to Joseph, taking no thought for anything himself except the food he ate (leaving all the management to Joseph); the complete confidence in the faithful steward; the high trust; Joseph's integrity proven (Potiphar at ease, all in Joseph's hand).

On "save the bread which he did eat," the witnesses note the freedom. Gill notes that Potiphar concerned himself with nothing but his food — so entirely did he trust Joseph; the steward's full charge; the master's ease.

On "Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the beauty: "Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured" — Joseph was handsome (in form and face, like his mother Rachel, 29:17); the comeliness that occasioned the temptation (v.7, Potiphar's wife casting her eyes on him); the note that prepares for the trial to come; the godly young man, fair and trusted, about to be tempted. The verse displays Potiphar's complete trust and Joseph's comeliness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the trust — "he left all that he had in Joseph's hand... save the bread which he did eat," Potiphar's complete confidence (Gill, Calvin); "save the bread which he did eat," the master's ease (Gill); "Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured," the comeliness (29:17; v.7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the trusted Joseph. The verse marks the trust: all in his hand. The verse displays the bread: only his care. The verse displays the comeliness: goodly. The verse displays the temptation: to come. The trust displays Joseph's integrity and the master's confidence. The verse displays the Potiphar: trusting. The verse displays the Joseph: fair. The verse displays the trial: at hand. Potiphar's complete trust and Joseph's comeliness display the integrity and the coming trial: Potiphar left all in Joseph's hand, and Joseph was goodly and well favoured (29:17) — the faithful steward fully trusted, the fair young man about to be tempted (v.7). The verse displays the comely Joseph, tempted by his master's wife (v.7).
7:
"And it came to pass after these things, that his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me." The witnesses note the temptation by Potiphar's wife. On "his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph," the grammatical voices note the temptation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me" — Potiphar's wife, taken with Joseph's beauty (v.6), looked upon him with lust and solicited him directly ("Lie with me"); the bold, shameless temptation; the trial of the young man (in a strange land, a slave, with the powerful mistress); the sin pressed upon him.

On "cast her eyes upon Joseph," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the lust. Henry and Calvin note that the woman "cast her eyes" (the lust of the eye, the beginning of the sin, as Eve, 3:6; David, 2 Samuel 11:2); the unchaste desire; the temptation arising from Joseph's comeliness; the snare.

On "Lie with me," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the solicitation: "Lie with me" — the bold, direct solicitation to adultery; the powerful temptation (the mistress, the opportunity, the danger of refusing); the trial that proved Joseph's chastity and fear of God (vv.8-9); the great temptation resisted. The verse displays the temptation by Potiphar's wife.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the temptation — "his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me," the bold solicitation (Keil, Gill); "cast her eyes upon Joseph," the lust of the eye (3:6; 2 Samuel 11:2, Henry, Calvin); "Lie with me," the direct temptation (vv.8-9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the tempting wife. The verse marks the eyes: cast on Joseph. The verse displays the solicitation: lie with me. The verse displays the lust: shameless. The verse displays the trial: of Joseph. The temptation displays the bold solicitation to sin. The verse displays the wife: tempting. The verse displays the Joseph: tempted. The verse displays the chastity: to be proved. The temptation by Potiphar's wife displays the great trial: she cast her eyes on Joseph and said, Lie with me (3:6) — the bold, shameless solicitation, the trial that would prove Joseph's chastity and fear of God (vv.8-9). The verse displays the tempted Joseph, who refuses (vv.8-9).
8:
"But he refused, and said unto his master's wife, Behold, my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand;" The witnesses note Joseph's refusal and his appeal to his master's trust. On "he refused," the grammatical voices note the refusal. Gill and Clarke note that "he refused, and said unto his master's wife" — Joseph refused the solicitation (v.7); the firm, immediate refusal; the chastity of the godly young man; the resisting of the temptation (despite the danger and the persistence, v.10); the steadfast refusal.

On "my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the trust. Clarke and Gill note Joseph's first reason — his master's trust: Potiphar knew not (took no account of) what was in the house, but had committed all to Joseph (v.6); to betray such trust would be base ingratitude and treachery; the appeal to the trust reposed in him; the faithfulness that would not wrong his master.

On the appeal, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the fidelity: Joseph's appeal to his master's complete trust (v.6) — the wrong of betraying it; the fidelity of the steward; the first ground of his refusal (the trust), leading to the higher ground (the sin against God, v.9); the honorable, faithful servant. The verse displays Joseph's refusal and his appeal to his master's trust.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refusal — "he refused, and said unto his master's wife," the firm refusal (v.7, Gill, Clarke); "my master... hath committed all that he hath to my hand," the trust appealed to (v.6, Clarke, Gill); the fidelity (v.9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the refusing Joseph. The verse marks the refusal: firm. The verse displays the trust: committed to him. The verse displays the fidelity: shown. The verse displays the sin: against the master. The refusal displays Joseph's chastity and fidelity. The verse displays the Joseph: refusing. The verse displays the master: trusting. The verse displays the wrong: of betrayal. Joseph's refusal and his appeal to his master's trust display his fidelity: he refused, for his master had committed all to his hand (v.6) — the firm refusal, the wrong of betraying such trust, leading to the higher ground (the sin against God, v.9). The verse displays the refusing Joseph, who names the sin against God (v.9).
9:
"There is none greater in this house than I; neither hath he kept back any thing from me but thee, because thou art his wife: how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?" The witnesses note Joseph's great refusal — "sin against God" — the crux of his chastity. On "There is none greater in this house than I... but thee, because thou art his wife," the grammatical voices note the wrong. Gill and JFB note that "none greater in this house than I; neither hath he kept back any thing from me but thee, because thou art his wife" — Joseph reasoned that his master had withheld nothing from him but his wife; to take her would be the basest treachery against the master who trusted him; the wrong against Potiphar; the betrayal of trust.

On "how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?" interpreters expound the ground. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note the great word — "how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?": Joseph's chief and highest ground for refusing — not merely the wrong to his master (real as that was), nor the fear of man, but the fear of God: the act would be a "great wickedness" and a "sin against God"; Joseph saw the adultery primarily as a sin against God (in whose sight it would be evil, Psalm 51:4, "against thee, thee only, have I sinned"); the fear of God the true root of his chastity; the conscience that would not sin against God even in secret (with none to see, in a strange land); the noblest motive for purity — that all sin is against God; the godly young man choosing rather to suffer (and he did suffer, vv.20) than to sin.

On the refusal, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the example: Joseph's "sin against God" is the model of true chastity and integrity — grounded in the fear of God, not in the fear of man or the chance of detection; the conscience awed by God's presence; the example for all (the secret of resisting temptation — to see it as sin against God); the noble refusal. The verse displays Joseph's great refusal — "sin against God."

The witnesses agree the substance: Joseph refused, seeing the act as a great wickedness and a sin against God (Gill, Wesley, JFB). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of his chastity: "how can I sin against God?" — Joseph's chief ground not the wrong to his master nor the fear of man, but the fear of God; the adultery seen primarily as a sin against God (Psalm 51:4), the conscience awed by God's presence even in secret; the fear of God the true root of chastity, the noblest motive for purity (all sin is against God). The verse displays the sin-against-God crux. The verse marks the wrong: against the master. The verse displays the higher ground: against God. The verse displays the fear: of God. The verse displays the chastity: rooted in God. The refusal displays Joseph's chastity grounded in the fear of God. The verse displays the Joseph: refusing. The verse displays the sin: against God. The verse displays the conscience: awed. Joseph's great refusal displays the crux: how can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God? (Psalm 51:4) — Joseph's chief ground the fear of God (not the wrong to his master nor the fear of man), the adultery seen as sin against God, the conscience awed even in secret, the fear of God the true root of chastity. The verse displays the refusing Joseph, pressed daily (v.10).
10:
"And it came to pass, as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her, or to be with her." The witnesses note Joseph's steadfast resistance to the daily temptation. On "as she spake to Joseph day by day," the grammatical voices note the persistence. Gill and Calvin note that "as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her" — Potiphar's wife persisted in her solicitation, day after day; the continued, persistent temptation; the trial prolonged; the snare pressed repeatedly; Joseph's steadfastness under the daily assault.

On "he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the resistance. Wesley and Gill note that Joseph "hearkened not" — he refused not only to lie with her but even "to be with her" (avoiding her company, the occasion of sin); the prudent avoidance; the steadfast resistance; the wisdom of fleeing the occasion (not parleying with the temptation).

On "or to be with her," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the prudence: "or to be with her" — Joseph avoided even being in her company (lest the temptation prevail); the prudent shunning of the occasion of sin; the steadfast, watchful resistance; the example of resisting persistent temptation by avoiding it (1 Corinthians 6:18, flee fornication). The verse displays Joseph's steadfast resistance to the daily temptation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the persistence — "as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her," the daily temptation resisted (Gill, Calvin); "he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her," the refusal (Wesley, Gill); "or to be with her," the prudent avoidance (1 Corinthians 6:18, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resisting Joseph. The verse marks the temptation: day by day. The verse displays the resistance: hearkened not. The verse displays the avoidance: not with her. The verse displays the steadfastness: shown. The resistance displays Joseph's steadfast, prudent refusal. The verse displays the Joseph: resisting. The verse displays the wife: persisting. The verse displays the occasion: avoided. Joseph's steadfast resistance to the daily temptation displays his prudent constancy: she spake day by day, yet he hearkened not, nor would be with her (1 Corinthians 6:18) — the persistent temptation steadfastly resisted, the prudent avoidance of the occasion of sin. The verse displays the steadfast Joseph, seized by the wife (vv.11-12).
11:
"And it came to pass about this time, that Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within." The very brief verse notes Joseph alone in the house, the occasion seized. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within" — Joseph went in to do his work (the steward's duties), and it happened that none of the household servants were there; the occasion (the empty house) that Potiphar's wife would seize (v.12); the providence (and the snare); Joseph attending to his duty.

On "to do his business," the witnesses note the diligence. Gill notes that Joseph went in "to do his business" — about his lawful work (the faithful steward); not seeking the occasion, but caught in it; the diligent servant.

On "there was none of the men of the house there within," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the snare: "there was none of the men of the house there within" — the empty house (the others away, perhaps by the wife's contrivance, at a festival, v.14); the opportunity for the temptation; the moment the wife would use (v.12); the trial at its height (Joseph alone, none to see — the test of his fear of God, v.9). The verse displays Joseph alone in the house.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within," Joseph alone (Keil, Gill); "to do his business," the diligent work (Gill); "there was none... there within," the snare (v.12, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the working Joseph. The verse marks the business: his work. The verse displays the house: empty. The verse displays the occasion: seized. The verse displays the snare: set. The occasion displays Joseph caught in the empty house. The verse displays the Joseph: at work. The verse displays the house: alone. The verse displays the temptation: at hand. Joseph alone in the house, the occasion seized, displays the trial at its height: Joseph went in to his work, and none of the men were there (v.9) — the empty house the wife would use (v.12), the test of his fear of God with none to see. The verse displays the alone Joseph, seized by the wife (v.12).
12:
"And she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me: and he left his garment in her hand, and fled, and got him out." The witnesses note Joseph fleeing, leaving his garment. On "she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me," the grammatical voices note the assault. Gill and Wesley note that "she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me" — Potiphar's wife, seizing the occasion (v.11), laid hold of Joseph's garment and pressed her solicitation; the bold, physical assault; the temptation at its most violent; the snare at its height.

On "he left his garment in her hand, and fled," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note the flight. Wesley and Gill note that Joseph "left his garment in her hand, and fled" — rather than yield, Joseph fled, leaving his garment in her grasp; the prompt, decisive flight from the temptation (the wisdom of fleeing, not parleying, 1 Corinthians 6:18, "flee fornication"; 2 Timothy 2:22); choosing the loss of his garment (and the danger of her wrath) over the loss of his innocence; the noble flight; the example of resisting temptation by flight.

On "and got him out," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the escape: "and fled, and got him out" — Joseph escaped the house; the decisive break from the sin; the chastity preserved (at the cost of the garment, which would be used to accuse him, vv.13-18); the godly young man fleeing the snare, trusting God with the consequences. The verse displays Joseph fleeing, leaving his garment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assault — "she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me," the violent temptation (Gill, Wesley); "he left his garment in her hand, and fled," the flight (1 Corinthians 6:18, Wesley, Gill); "and got him out," the escape (vv.13-18, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fleeing Joseph. The verse marks the assault: caught his garment. The verse displays the flight: left it and fled. The verse displays the chastity: preserved. The verse displays the garment: the evidence. The flight displays Joseph escaping the snare. The verse displays the Joseph: fleeing. The verse displays the garment: left behind. The verse displays the innocence: kept. Joseph fleeing, leaving his garment, displays the noble flight from temptation: she caught his garment, but he left it and fled (1 Corinthians 6:18; 2 Timothy 2:22) — choosing the loss of his garment over his innocence, the decisive flight from the snare. The verse displays the fled Joseph, falsely accused by the wife (vv.13-18).
13:
"And it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth," The very brief verse notes the wife's malice turning at Joseph's flight. The grammatical voices note the turn. Gill and Henry note that "when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth" — Potiphar's wife, seeing Joseph fled and his garment in her hand, turned from lust to malice (the scorned woman's spite); the love (lust) turned to hatred; the occasion of her false accusation (vv.14-18); the garment now her weapon.

On "he had left his garment in her hand," the witnesses note the evidence. Gill notes that the garment (left in flight, v.12) became the false evidence — the wife using it to accuse Joseph (as if he had assaulted her, vv.15-18); the innocent token turned to a lie; the spite of the rejected woman.

On "and was fled forth," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the malice: "and was fled forth" — Joseph's flight (his innocence) provoked the woman's malice; the scorned lust turning to revenge (as Amnon's, 2 Samuel 13:15, love to hatred); the false accusation to follow (vv.14-18); the godly man's integrity answered with slander. The verse displays the wife's malice turning at Joseph's flight.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 14:
"That she called unto the men of her house, and spake unto them, saying, See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us; he came in unto me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice:" The witnesses note the wife's false accusation against Joseph. On "she called unto the men of her house... See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us," the grammatical voices note the slander. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she called unto the men of her house... See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us" — the wife, turning her malice to slander, called the household servants and accused Joseph (the "Hebrew," with contempt; blaming even her husband, "he hath brought in," for bringing the Hebrew); the false charge — that Joseph had come to "mock" (insult, abuse) them; the lie inverting the truth (she the tempter, he the innocent).

On "he came in unto me to lie with me," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the inversion. Clarke and Keil note the false accusation — that Joseph "came in unto me to lie with me" (the very thing she had solicited, v.7, now laid on him); the lie reversing the roles; the slander against the innocent; the false charge of assault.

On "I cried with a loud voice," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the pretense: "I cried with a loud voice" — the wife claimed she had cried out (the proof, by law, of a woman's resistance, Deuteronomy 22:24, 27); the false witness; the garment (v.15) the supposed evidence; the slander framed to convict Joseph; the innocent accused. The verse displays the wife's false accusation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the slander — "he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us... he came in unto me to lie with me," the false accusation (Keil, Gill); "an Hebrew... to mock us," the contemptuous charge (Clarke, Keil); "I cried with a loud voice," the pretended resistance (Deuteronomy 22:24, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the accusing wife. The verse marks the slander: the false charge. The verse displays the Hebrew: with contempt. The verse displays the lie: inverted. The verse displays the lying wife, who keeps the garment (v.15-18).
15:
"And it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled, and got him out." The very brief verse notes the wife's lie about Joseph's garment. The grammatical voices note the fabrication. Gill notes that "when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled" — the wife's fabricated story: that Joseph, hearing her cry, fled and left his garment (twisting the true fact, v.12, that he fled to escape her, into a proof of his guilt); the lie built on the garment; the false account to the servants (and to Potiphar, vv.17-18); the slander framed.

On "he left his garment with me," the witnesses note the evidence. Gill notes that the garment (left in Joseph's flight, v.12) was the wife's evidence — twisted to prove he had assaulted her (and fled when she cried); the innocent token turned to a lie; the proof fabricated.

On "and fled, and got him out," the grammatical-historical note the inversion: "and fled, and got him out" — the true fact (Joseph fled to escape sin, v.12) inverted into a sign of guilt (fleeing when caught); the slander complete; the innocent's flight used against him; the false witness. The verse displays the wife's lie about Joseph's garment.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. On "laid up his garment by her," the witnesses note the preparation. Gill notes that the wife kept the garment as her evidence — the deliberate, calculated slander; the proof readied for Potiphar; the malice premeditated.

On "until his lord came home," the grammatical-historical note the accusation: "until his lord came home" — the wife awaited Potiphar to lay the charge (vv.17-18); the false accusation prepared; the innocent Joseph to be condemned; the slander brought to the husband. The verse displays the wife keeping the garment for Potiphar.

19:
"And it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife, which she spake unto him, saying, After this manner did thy servant to me; that his wrath was kindled." The witnesses note Potiphar's wrath kindled by his wife's accusation. On "when his master heard the words of his wife... that his wrath was kindled," the grammatical voices note the anger. Gill and Calvin note that "when his master heard the words of his wife... that his wrath was kindled" — Potiphar, hearing his wife's accusation (vv.17-18), was angry; his wrath kindled (against Joseph, believing the false charge); the master deceived by the lie; the anger that led to Joseph's imprisonment (v.20).

On "After this manner did thy servant to me," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the charge. Henry and Gill note the wife's words — "after this manner did thy servant to me" (the false charge of assault); the slander that provoked Potiphar; the innocent servant accused; the lie believed.

On "his wrath was kindled," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: Potiphar's wrath (yet, the witnesses note, his comparatively mild punishment — prison, not death, v.20 — may suggest some doubt of Joseph's guilt, or restraint by God); the anger that brought Joseph low (the false accusation, the trial of his faith); the providence overruling even the injustice (the prison the way to the palace, ch. 41); the innocent suffering wrongfully (1 Peter 2:19). The verse displays Potiphar's wrath kindled by his wife's accusation.

20:
"And Joseph's master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king's prisoners were bound: and he was there in the prison." The witnesses note Joseph cast into prison. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph's master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king's prisoners were bound" — Potiphar imprisoned Joseph (in the royal prison, where the king's prisoners were kept — the prison attached to the captain of the guard's house, 40:3); the innocent cast into prison on the false charge (vv.17-19); the godly young man suffering wrongfully; the further descent (from steward to prisoner).

On "a place where the king's prisoners were bound," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the prison. Clarke and Keil note the royal prison (where state prisoners were held, 40:3); Joseph bound there (Psalm 105:18, "whose feet they hurt with fetters: he was laid in iron"); the harsh imprisonment; the trial of the innocent.

On "he was there in the prison," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: "he was there in the prison" — Joseph in the prison (yet the LORD with him there, v.21); the depth of his trial (sold, enslaved, falsely accused, imprisoned); the providence overruling — the prison the place where Joseph would meet the butler and baker (ch. 40) and be brought to Pharaoh (ch. 41); the innocent suffering, by God's hand, on the way to exaltation (Psalm 105:17-22). The verse displays Joseph cast into prison.

21:
"But the LORD was with Joseph, and shewed him mercy, and gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison." The witnesses note the LORD being with Joseph in the prison. On "the LORD was with Joseph," the grammatical voices note the presence. JFB and Gill note that "the LORD was with Joseph, and shewed him mercy" — even in the prison, the LORD was with Joseph (the keynote again, v.2); God's gracious presence in the depth of his trial; the comfort and mercy of God to the innocent sufferer; the "but" marking the contrast (imprisoned, yet not forsaken — the LORD with him); the faithfulness of God to his servant.

On "shewed him mercy," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the kindness. Wesley and Gill note that God "shewed him mercy" (kindness, covenant-love) — comforting and helping Joseph in the prison; the mercy of God to the afflicted; the kindness that sustained him; the LORD's tender care.

On "gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the favor: "gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison" — God gave Joseph favor with the prison-keeper (as with Potiphar, v.4); the LORD inclining the keeper's heart to Joseph; the providence raising Joseph even in the prison (to be trusted with the prisoners, v.22); the favor of God turning the prison to a place of trust. 23:

The verse displays the LORD prospering Joseph in the prison.
